evals.query: I want to build an AI agent that handles customer support — looking up orders, processing refunds, answering product questions. Where do I start?
expected_behavior: Recommends starting with a single agent, not jumping to multi-agent
Suggests routing as a potential pattern if query types are distinct (orders vs refunds vs product questions)
Discusses keeping the tool set small (10-20 tools max)
Mentions progressive disclosure for knowledge base access
Identifies human-in-the-loop checkpoints for refund approval
Advises measuring before adding complexity

evals.query: My coding agent works fine on small tasks but falls apart on large refactors that touch 20+ files. It loses track of what it's done and starts repeating work or contradicting earlier changes. How do I fix this?
expected_behavior: Identifies context window exhaustion as the core problem
Recommends the Ralph Loop pattern for long-running tasks
Describes the initializer → plan file → worker loop → verification flow
Suggests offloading context to filesystem (plan.md, progress.md)
Mentions git as memory — each commit is a checkpoint
Discusses stop hooks for verification after each iteration
Does NOT just suggest 'use a bigger context window'

evals.query: I'm building a research agent that needs to gather information from 10 different sources, analyze findings, and write a report. Should I use one agent or multiple?
expected_behavior: Recommends parallelization for the gathering phase (10 sources can be queried concurrently)
Suggests an orchestrator-workers or coordinator pattern for the overall flow
Distinguishes between the parallel gathering and sequential analysis/synthesis phases
Discusses context isolation — each source-gathering sub-agent has focused context
Mentions aggregating results before the analysis phase
Warns that multi-agent adds cost and complexity — verify single agent can't handle it first

evals.query: My agent has 47 MCP tools loaded and it keeps picking the wrong ones. Latency is also terrible. What's the right approach?
expected_behavior: Identifies too many tools as the core problem — agents work best with 10-20
Recommends progressive disclosure: index tool definitions, retrieve on demand
Suggests the multi-layer action space: push actions from tool layer to computer (bash, code execution)
Discusses token savings from code execution vs tool call chains
May suggest consolidating overlapping tools (links to designing-agent-tools skill)
Addresses latency — fewer tools in context means faster model inference

evals.query: I need to build a content pipeline: generate blog post → edit for style → check facts → SEO optimize → format for CMS. What pattern should I use?
expected_behavior: Identifies this as a fixed sequential workflow — steps are known in advance
Recommends prompt chaining or sequential multi-agent pattern
Suggests adding programmatic gates between steps (quality checks)
May recommend evaluator-optimizer loop for the edit step specifically
Does NOT suggest a coordinator or swarm pattern — task is structured, not open-ended
Notes the trade-off: latency increases with each step but accuracy improves

evals.query: How should I handle context for a production agent that runs for 30+ minutes on complex tasks? My current approach just keeps appending to the message history until it hits the limit.
expected_behavior: Identifies context growth as the core issue — models degrade with more context
Recommends context offloading to filesystem as first strategy (not summarization)
Explains the offload-first principle: offload → index → summarize+offload → summarize-only
Discusses prompt caching and its importance for cost (cache hit rate as key metric)
Suggests the Ralph Loop for tasks that truly exceed one context window
Mentions plan files for steering and periodic re-reading to prevent drift
Warns against summarize-first approach — information loss

evals.query: We want our agent to get better over time — learn from past sessions, remember what worked, avoid repeating mistakes. How do we architect this?
expected_behavior: Describes context evolution as the framework — updating agent context (not model weights)
Explains the trajectory reflection pattern: collect sessions → score → reflect → update
Distinguishes between task-specific prompt optimization and open-ended memory learning
Mentions skill discovery: distilling reusable procedures from trajectories into skill files
References the sleep-time pattern for offline context consolidation
Grounds recommendations in practical implementation (memory files, CLAUDE.md updates)
Does NOT suggest fine-tuning as the primary approach

evals.query: I need to build an agent that can work autonomously on a large project for hours — implementing 50+ features in a web app. It can't fit in one context window. How do I architect this?
expected_behavior: Recommends the two-agent architecture: initializer agent + worker agent loop
Describes what the initializer creates: setup script, feature list (JSON), progress file, initial git commit
Describes the worker session protocol: read state → verify baseline → implement one feature → test → commit → update progress
Emphasizes JSON over markdown for state files (less corruption risk)
Mentions the 'only modify passes field' rule to prevent agents from deleting tests
Explains why workers must verify existing features before starting new work
Notes that git history serves as memory across sessions
